title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Telling the Truth: The Art of Documentary Filmmaking
content.text: In 1922, Robert Flaherty spent months living with an Inuit family in northern Canada, filming their daily life and survival routines. The result, Nanook of the North, is widely regarded as the first feature-length documentary. (37) [37] Aunque algunas escenas fueron recreadas, la película estableció los principios fundamentales del género. [/37] Ever since, documentary makers have wrestled with the same core tension: how to tell a compelling story while remaining truthful to their subject. || The tools available to documentary filmmakers have changed beyond recognition. Where Flaherty needed bulky equipment and a large crew, a contemporary filmmaker can capture broadcast-quality footage on a device that fits in a pocket. (38) [38] Esta democratización tecnológica ha ampliado enormemente quién puede hacer documentales y sobre qué temas. [/38] Voices and stories that were once inaccessible to professional crews — because of geography, cost or politics — can now be captured by filmmakers working alone with minimal resources. || Choosing a subject is only the beginning. The real challenge lies in building the trust required to gain genuine, unguarded access to people's lives. (39) [39] Los documentalistas más hábiles pueden pasar meses o años ganándose la confianza de sus protagonistas antes de encender la cámara. [/39] Without that foundation of trust, subjects perform for the camera rather than simply living in front of it, and the film loses its power. || The editing room is where a documentary is truly made. Hours of raw footage — sometimes thousands of hours — must be shaped into a coherent narrative that holds an audience's attention. (40) [40] El montador es, en muchos sentidos, el coautor invisible del documental. [/40] Decisions about what to include, what to omit and how to sequence events can fundamentally alter the viewer's perception of reality. || The ethical responsibilities of documentary filmmakers are significant. Subjects may not fully understand how they will be portrayed, and the power imbalance between filmmaker and subject can be exploited, deliberately or not. (41) [41] Algunos documentalistas se han enfrentado a críticas severas por manipular la realidad o perjudicar a sus protagonistas. [/41] The industry has responded with ethical guidelines and codes of practice, though enforcement remains patchy and contested. || Despite these complexities, the documentary form continues to expand its reach and influence. (42) [42] Las plataformas de streaming han dado a los documentales una audiencia global sin precedentes. [/42] Films that might once have reached a few thousand viewers at a festival circuit now attract millions of viewers within days of release, giving them the power to shift public opinion and prompt political action.

paragraphs.A: Although some scenes were staged, the film established the essential principles of the documentary form.
paragraphs.B: The editor is, in many respects, the unseen co-author of any documentary film.
paragraphs.C: Streaming platforms have given documentary films a global reach that was previously unimaginable.
paragraphs.D: The relationship between a filmmaker and their subjects evolves continually throughout the production process.
paragraphs.E: Some documentary makers have faced serious criticism for distorting reality or causing harm to the people they filmed.
paragraphs.F: This technological democratisation has opened the form to a far wider range of filmmakers and subjects.
paragraphs.G: Skilled documentary makers may spend months or even years earning the trust of their subjects before filming begins.

questions.explanation: A acknowledges the limitations of Nanook but highlights its foundational role in the genre, connecting with the narrative tension described below.
F explains the impact of the democratisation of technology on access to documentary film, preparing the examples of new voices and geographies.
G details the time required to earn the trust of the subjects, directly connecting with the consequence of not having it.
B introduces the editor as an invisible co-author, before explaining the enormous power of editing decisions over the viewer's perception.
E specifies the criticisms some documentary filmmakers have received for manipulation or harm to their subjects, linking to the sector's response.
C points out the role of streaming platforms in the expansion of the documentary, preparing the contrast with the previous reach of the festival circuit.
